perturbation.original_text: All information, concerning the business of the other Party, hereafter in the possession of either of theParties, or their respective directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives, including, withoutlimitation, lawyers, accountants, consultants and financial advisors (collectively, "Representatives") andexcept as noted hereinafter is considered to be "Confidential Information".
perturbation.changed_text: All information, concerning the business of the other Party, hereafter in the possession of either of theParties, or their respective directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives, including, withoutlimitation, lawyers, accountants, consultants and financial advisors (collectively, "Representatives") is considered 'Proprietary Information'. Proprietary Information will only be protected when deemed such by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Proprietary Information', and the stringent requirements for it to be considered confidential are removed, making the term less precise and its protection discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's 'Trade Secrets Act' protection of confidential information hinges on reasonable measures to maintain secrecy, not solely on the disclosing party's discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows the disclosing party to decide what is 'Proprietary Information' at their own discretion, which is contrary to the general legal expectation that reasonable efforts are made to protect information independently of simply claiming it to be so.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Confidential Information' with specific criteria. The changed text replaces this with 'Proprietary Information' and allows the disclosing party sole discretion in determining what qualifies. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the legal expectation of reasonable efforts to protect information, independent of simply claiming it to be so. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, where the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The scraped snippet does not provide any conflicting information so I am able to conclude that the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations stipulated by this agreement shall continue in force for _____ years from the date ofsigning, unless otherwise extended or superceded by mutual agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations stipulated by this agreement shall continue in force indefinitely from the date of signing, unless terminated at will by either party.
perturbation.explanation: The contract now allows indefinite duration, contradicted by common law contract principles requiring reasonable limitations for non-compete and confidentiality clauses, even impliedly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law principles regarding reasonable limitations on restrictive covenants.
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Contract Law - Reasonableness of Restrictive Covenants
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario's common law requires restrictive covenants to be reasonable in scope and duration. Removing a time limit and adding 'terminated at will by either party' makes the obligations indefinite and potentially unreasonable, contradicting common law principles for contract enforceability regarding restrictive covenants.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Einführung Einer Schweizer Investitionskontrolle – Bundesrat Veröffentlicht Vorentwurf - Inward/ Foreign Investment - Switzerland ARTICLE 25 May 2022 Einführung Einer Schweizer Investitionskontrolle – Bundesrat Veröffentlicht Vorentwurf LS Lenz & Staehelin More Contributor With over 200 lawyers and offices in three major economic centres, Lenz & Staehelin is one of the largest law firms in Switzerland. Known for its high professional standards, the firm provides its domestic and international clients with a full range of services in the main areas of law. It is ranked amongst the leading practitioners in business law. Explore Firm Details Im Auftrag des Parlaments hat der Bundesrat einen Vorentwurf zur Vernehmlassung publiziert, der die Einführung einer Schweizer Investitionskontrolle vorsieht. Switzerland Government, Public Sector Marcel Meinhardt, Astrid Waser, Sevan Antreasyan +1 Authors Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Im Auftrag des Parlaments hat der Bundesrat einen Vorentwurf zur Vernehmlassung publiziert, der die Einführung einer Schweizer Investitionskontrolle vorsieht. Mit der geplanten Investitionskontrolle folgt die Schweiz einem internationalen Trend zu schärferer Kontrolle von ausländischen Investitionen. Ausgangslage Die Schweiz zählt zu den weltweit grössten Empfängern von ausländischen Investitionen, verfügt bislang jedoch noch über keine übergreifende Regelung zur Prüfung von ausländischen Direktinvestitionen. Mit dem eben publizierten Vorentwurf soll nun auch in der Schweiz eine zielgerichtete und administrativ schlanke Investitionskontrolle eingeführt werden. Genehmigungspflichte Übernahmen Der Vorentwurf sieht eine sektorübergreifende und eine sektorspezifische Prüfung von Übernahmen vor, wobei für alle Übernahmen eine de minimis Ausnahme bestehen soll. Im Rahmen der sektorübergreifenden Prüfung sollen Übernahmen durch ausländische Investoren, die unmittelbar oder mittelbar von einer staatlichen Stelle kontrolliert werden, erfasst werden. Eine sektorspezifische Prüfung soll für Übernahmen durch ausländische Investoren allgemein (d.h. insbesondere auch durch private ausländische Investoren) eingeführt werden. Dabei wird unterschieden zwischen Übernahmen (i) von Unternehmen in klassischen sicherheitssrelevanten Sektoren wie der Lieferung von Rüstungsgütern, dem Betrieb inländischer Kraftwerke oder der Lieferung von zentralen sicherheitsrelevanten IT-Systemen für schweizerische Behörden und (ii) von Unternehmen in anderen Bereichen, in denen Risiken für die öffentliche Ordnung oder Sicherheit nicht gänzlich ausgeschlossen werden können. Dazu zählen etwa Universitätsspitäler oder Unternehmen im Bereich der Forschung, Entwicklung, Produktion und des Vertriebs von Arzneimitteln oder Impfstoffen. Für letztere (ii) ist eine Umsatzschwelle von 100 Millionen Franken vorgesehen, ab welcher Transaktionen gemeldet werden müssen. Eine generelle Ausnahme (Bagatellschwelle) besteht für die Übernahme von kleinen inländischen Unternehmen (weniger als 50 Vollzeitstellen und weltweiter Jahresumsatz weniger als 10 Millionen Franken in den vergangenen zwei Geschäftsjahren). Meldepflichtig sind gemäss dem Vorentwurf nur Übernahmen, durch die ein Investor unmittelbar oder mittelbar die Kontrolle über ein Unternehmen oder Teile davon erlangt. Diese Definition orientiert sich am kartellrechtlichen Kontrollbegriff. Minderheitsbeteiligungen ohne Kontrollerwerb werden somit nicht erfasst. Noch unklar ist, ob auch die Übernahme von inländischen Tochterunternehmen ausländischer Unternehmensgruppen unter den Geltungsbereich fallen sollen. Im Vorentwurf ist ausserdem vorgesehen, dass der Bundesrat Übernahmen durch ausländische Investoren aus bestimmten Staaten vom Geltungsbereich ausnehmen kann, sofern die öffentliche Ordnung und Sicherheit gewährleistet ist. Genehmigungsverfahren Der Vorentwurf sieht ein zweistufiges Genehmigungsverfahren vor. Nachdem der aus
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulated a fixed duration, while the modified version allows for an indefinite duration terminable at will. The Perturbation and Law explanations point out that Ontario common law requires restrictive covenants to be reasonable in scope and duration. Although no direct law snippet confirms this specifically, the nature of the change from a defined period to indefinite, combined with the explanation that this is an issue related to 'restrictive covenants' implies that there is a contradiction, since legally, restrictive covenants need to be reasonable.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario,Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Quebec, Canada, with decisions made at the sole discretion of the First Party.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement now specifies Quebec law but adds that decisions are at the sole discretion of the First Party. This contradicts Quebec's civil law principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Quebec Civil Code's principles of good faith and fairness in contractual interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Quebec's Civil Code mandates good faith in contracts (Art. 1375). Granting the First Party sole discretion on decisions contradicts this principle, as it implies potential for biased or unfair interpretations of the agreement, which is not allowed under Quebec law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text designates Quebec law as governing but then grants sole discretion to the First Party, potentially allowing for decisions that are not in good faith. The Law Explanation states that Quebec's Civil Code mandates good faith in contracts (Art. 1375), and giving the First Party sole discretion introduces a contradiction by suggesting potential bias and unfairness, which is not compliant with Quebec law. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, because the term 'sole discretion' clashes with the underlying principle of good faith required by Quebec law, making the contract potentially unenforceable under that jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that the other Party would not have an adequate remedy at lawand would be irreparably harmed if any of the provisions of this Agreement were not performed inaccordance with their specific terms or were otherwise breached. Accordingly, the Parties agree thateither Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent breaches of this Agreement and to specificallyenforce the terms and provisions hereof in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled at lawor in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that monetary damages are the sole remedy for any breach of this agreement. Injunctive relief is explicitly waived by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The contract now limits the remedy to monetary damages, explicitly waiving injunctive relief, even if monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law regarding availability of equitable remedies when legal remedies are inadequate.
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Equitable Remedies in Contract Law - Ontario
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can contractually limit remedies, a complete waiver of equitable remedies like injunctive relief may be unenforceable if monetary damages are demonstrably inadequate to address the harm caused by a breach, especially in cases involving unique or irreplaceable assets, contravening basic principles of contract law regarding ensuring adequate remedy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly waives injunctive relief, limiting the remedy to monetary damages, which contradicts the general principle that parties are entitled to adequate remedies, including equitable remedies like injunctive relief when monetary damages are insufficient. The Law Explanation clearly states this potential unenforceability. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily concerns court procedures, it implicitly acknowledges the court's role in providing remedies, which the changed text attempts to restrict. This restriction can be seen as undermining the court's ability to ensure adequate remedy, leading to a legal contradiction.
